Wagon Windows covers Salmon Arm and the full Shuswap region

faqs.q: Why are my windows staining so much faster in Salmon Arm than in my previous home in Vancouver?
faqs.a: The primary reason is water hardness. Vancouver's water supply comes from mountain reservoirs fed by snowmelt and rainfall — it's extremely soft, typically 10–25 mg/L of dissolved minerals. Salmon Arm's municipal water runs three to six times harder, at 80–120 mg/L. If you're on well water in the rural Shuswap, the gap is even larger. Each time water touches your windows and evaporates, it leaves behind a proportional amount of dissolved minerals. With three to six times the mineral content per water contact, Salmon Arm windows accumulate mineral deposits at three to six times the rate. Add to that the dry interior BC summer — fewer rain events to dilute deposits, more irrigation contact with hard tap or well water — and the conditions for rapid staining are significantly more pronounced than coastal BC homeowners are accustomed to.

faqs.q: Do I need a water softener if I live in Salmon Arm to protect my windows?
faqs.a: A whole-home water softener will address hard water staining on windows in areas where the staining source is indoor water use or plumbing-related water contact. However, the most common cause of exterior window staining in Salmon Arm — irrigation contact — typically uses unsoftened water from your main supply anyway, and most whole-home softeners don't process outdoor irrigation lines. If your staining is driven by irrigation spray hitting windows, a water softener won't address that source. The more direct solutions are repositioning irrigation heads to stop spraying toward the house and applying a protective hydrophobic coating to the glass. Water softeners genuinely help with interior windows, fixture scaling, appliance longevity, and general water quality — they're worth considering on well water — but they're not the primary lever for exterior window staining prevention in most Salmon Arm homes.

faqs.q: How often should Salmon Arm homeowners get their windows professionally cleaned?
faqs.a: For most Salmon Arm homes on municipal water with standard landscaping, once per year — spring, after pollen season — is the practical minimum to prevent mineral buildup from progressing to the bonded deposit stage. This single annual clean should include hard water assessment so that any staining that accumulated through the previous season is caught and treated before it advances. For homes with irrigation systems that contact windows, or for homes on well water, twice per year — spring and fall — is the recommended interval. The fall clean addresses summer mineral accumulation before winter prevents treatment. For lake-adjacent properties with regular spray exposure, the twice-yearly interval is important because boating season spray adds a second peak accumulation period on top of standard irrigation staining. If you apply a professional-grade protective coating after a clean and hard water treatment, some homeowners in lower-exposure situations can extend to once yearly — but only with the coating actively maintaining protection.

faqs.q: Is the water harder in Salmon Arm than in Kelowna or Kamloops?
faqs.a: Municipal water hardness across these Interior BC cities is broadly comparable, with some seasonal and source variation. Kelowna's municipal supply typically runs 75–100 mg/L — similar to Salmon Arm's 80–120 mg/L range. Kamloops is generally slightly softer, around 60–80 mg/L from Thompson River sources. Vernon and Armstrong, drawing from similar geological sources as the Shuswap, are comparable to Salmon Arm. None of these cities has dramatically harder or softer municipal water than the others — they're all in the moderately hard to hard range that represents a significant step up from coastal BC. The bigger variable within the Shuswap region itself is the well water picture: rural properties in the Shuswap valley can have groundwater significantly harder than any of these municipal supplies, pushing some homeowners into the very hard category (180+ mg/L) that produces the most aggressive window and appliance mineral buildup.
